title: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Important Entities
exclusions: Does not classify the entity's NIS2 scope (essential vs important), interpret national transposition thresholds for important entities, or replace counsel determination. Only surfaces risk evidence that an auditor can review for important-entity obligations.
evidence: `UC-22.2.22` saved search output, Qualys correlation notes, and signed CSV extracts showing `host`, `aged_findings`, captured in `audit_evidence` for important-entity backlog review.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2) is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.22: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Important Entities.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.22' running on nis2_entity_classification.csv (entity_tier=important), qualys:hostdetection, authentication CIM, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Mark `important-host-01` in the lookup, ingest Qualys events with `severity=High`, `first_found_datetime` 45 days old, rerun: expect the host in results with `aged_findings>=1`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Patch the issue so Qualys clears the detection or refresh `first_found_datetime` inside 30 days and rerun the same window: expect zero matching rows for that host when SLA is met.

dataSources: `index=qualys` `sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection` exposes `qid`, `severity`, `first_found_datetime`, `host`. Important-tier assets are filtered via `nis2_entity_classification.csv` columns `asset_hostname`, `business_service`, `entity_tier`. Supplemental context can be pulled from `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:risk` (`risk_id`, `likelihood`) and `index=auth` with `sourcetype=OktaIM2:log` for access spikes. Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964); Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) optional for dashboards.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928)
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv WHERE entity_tier="important"
| rename asset_hostname as host
| join host type=left [
    search index=qualys sourcetype="qualys:hostdetection" earliest=-30d
    | eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(first_found_datetime,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))/86400,0)
    | where severity IN ("Critical","High") AND age_days > 30
    | stats dc(qid) as aged_findings by host
]
| where isnotnull(aged_findings) AND aged_findings > 0
| table host, business_service, aged_findings
| sort - aged_findings
description: Flags important-tier hosts whose critical or high Qualys findings are older than the proportionate SLA, proving lighter-weight but time-bound risk analysis stays honest without essential-tier overhead.
value: Supports fair regulatory storytelling for important entities by translating proportionality into concrete telemetry—aged backlog counts by `business_service`—so teams can explain why controls are right-sized, not merely absent.
implementation: (1) Maintain `nis2_entity_classification.csv` with accurate hostnames for important entities. (2) Keep Qualys connector (Splunkbase 2964) healthy; alert on ingest breaks separately. (3) Tune `age_days > 30` to national risk policy. (4) Export monthly management review decks from Splunk. (5) Sync exceptions through `nis2_qualys_exception_register.csv` with expiry.
visualization: Bar chart of aged findings by host, table of worst offenders, single value counting hosts past SLA.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
• **Qualys scanning paused on satellite plants** — Distinguish: detectors show aged `first_found_datetime` because no fresh scan arrived. Suppress: join `nis2_scan_blackout.csv` with `scan_paused=true` for the host during the outage window.
• **Accepted qualys QIDs** — Distinguish: backlog is intentional with compensating control tickets. Suppress: lookup `nis2_qid_exception_register.csv` listing `(host,qid)` pairs with `approved_until`.
• **Hostname case mismatch** — Distinguish: Qualys emits `HOST.EXAMPLE` while CSV uses lower case. Suppress: normalize with `| eval host=lower(host)` before the join and in the lookup staging query.
• **Transient cloud ephemerals** — Distinguish: autoscale nodes disappear mid-cycle. Suppress: filter `| lookup nis2_cloud_transient.csv host OUTPUT transient | where transient!="yes"`.
• **Reclassification from important to essential** — Distinguish: stale CSV still lists `entity_tier=important`. Suppress: weekly automated reload from ServiceNow CMDB via Splunkbase 1928 export job.
equipmentModels: Qualys Cloud Platform (VMDR module, host detection exports)
Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, vulnerability detection via Qualys API)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, optional GRC risk register context)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, optional asset model and dashboards)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `qualys` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_entity_classification.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_entity_classification.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.22: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Important Entities." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `qualys` under `qualys:hostdetection`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `nis2_entity_classification.csv` with accurate hostnames for important entities. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Keep Qualys connector (Splunkbase 2964) healthy; alert on ingest breaks separately. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** Tune `age_days > 30` to national risk policy. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 4:** Export monthly management review decks from Splunk. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 5:** Sync exceptions through `nis2_qualys_exception_register.csv` with expiry. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Important Entities," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `nis2_entity_classification.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup nis2_entity_classification.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true nis2_entity_classification_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 30 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Vulnerabilities` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Vulnerabilities` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.22: NIS2 Risk Analysis Evidence for Important Entities]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.22,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=qualys sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `aged_findings`, `business_service`, `host`. Run:

```spl
index=qualys sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, aged_findings, business_service, host
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `age_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(age_days) as p50 perc90(age_days) as p90 perc99(age_days) as p99 max(age_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.22: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Bar Chart Of Aged Findings By Host (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Worst Offenders (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value Counting Hosts Past Sla (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: For the slightly-smaller-but-still-regulated group, we watch whether serious known software problems are left sitting too long, so we can say our follow-up matches the lighter rules that apply to them.
